title: Q# namespaces and open directives
content: Q# programs consist of namespace blocks. All callable and type declarations must be inside a namespace; there are no top-level declarations outside a namespace. Each file can contain multiple namespaces, but namespaces do not nest. Use open directives inside the namespace to access callables from other namespaces; either qualify names or open the namespace. Namespaces are not hierarchical even if they share a prefix.

title: Callable declarations and functors
content: Q# supports two callable kinds: operations (can have side effects) and functions (deterministic, no side effects). Operations can declare functor support with `is Adj + Ctl` and the compiler can generate adjoint/controlled specializations when valid. Functions have only a single body. Callables can be recursive and can be declared in any order within a namespace.

title: Q# type system overview
content: Q# has a small immutable type system: Unit, Int, BigInt, Double, Bool, String, Qubit, Result, Pauli, Range, arrays, tuples, and user-defined types. Arrays are homogeneous sequences. Tuples are fixed-size groupings of values. Operations and functions are first-class callable types with tuple-valued input and output. Prefer explicit type annotations in callable signatures.

title: Q# expressions and modifiers
content: Expressions are composed of literals, identifiers, operators, and callable invocations. Modifiers like functor application are prefix operators applied to callables. Item access expressions and tuple/array literals are common building blocks. Use operations for quantum effects and functions for pure computation.
